name: The necessity of stability
statement_text:
A general linear method (A, U, B, V ) is convergent only if
it is stable.
proof_text:
Proof. Suppose, on the contrary, that { V n : n = 1, 2, 3, . . . } is unbounded.
This implies that there exists a sequence of vectors w1 , w2 , w3 , . . . such that
 wn = 1, for all n = 1, 2, 3, . . . , and such that the sequence { V n wn : n =
1, 2, 3, . . . } is unbounded. Consider the solution of the initial value problem

                              y  (x) = 0,       y(0) = 0,

using (A, U, B, V ), where n steps are taken with stepsize h = 1/n, so that the
solution is approximated at x = 1. Irrespective of the choice of the vector u
in Definition 512A, the convergence of the method implies that the sequence
of approximations converges to zero. For the approximation carried out with
n steps, use as the starting approximation
                            1          1
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
Suppose, on the contrary, that $\{ V^{n} : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \}$ is unbounded.
This implies that there exists a sequence of vectors $w_{1}, w_{2}, w_{3}, \dots$ such that
$\| w_{n} \| = 1$, for all $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, and such that the sequence $\{ V^{n} w_{n} : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \}$ is unbounded.
Consider the solution of the initial value problem
\[
y'(x) = 0, \quad y(0) = 0,
\]
using $(A, U, B, V)$, where $n$ steps are taken with stepsize $h = 1/n$, so that the
solution is approximated at $x = 1$. Irrespective of the choice of the vector $u$
in Definition~\ref{def:512A}, the convergence of the method implies that the sequence
of approximations converges to zero. For the approximation carried out with
$n$ steps, use as the starting approximation
\[
\phi\left( \frac{1}{n} \right) = \frac{1}{\max_{i=1}^{n} \| V^{i} w_{i} \|} w_{n}.
\]
This converges to zero, because $\| \phi(1/n) \| = \left( \max_{i=1}^{n} \| V^{i} w_{i} \| \right)^{-1}$. The result,
computed after $n$ steps, will then be
\[
V^{n} \phi\left( \frac{1}{n} \right) = \frac{1}{\max_{i=1}^{n} \| V^{i} w_{i} \|} V^{n} w_{n},
\]
with norm
\[
\left\| V^{n} \phi\left( \frac{1}{n} \right) \right\| = \frac{\| V^{n} w_{n} \|}{\max_{i=1}^{n} \| V^{i} w_{i} \|}. \tag{513a}
\]
Because the sequence $n \to \| V^{n} w_{n} \|$ is unbounded, an infinite set of $n$ values
will have the property that the maximum value of $\| V^{i} w_{i} \|$, for $i \leq n$, will
occur with $i = n$. This means that \eqref{eq:513a} has value $1$ arbitrarily often, and
hence is not convergent to zero as $n \to \infty$.
\end{proof}
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
A general linear method is defined by matrices $(A, U, B, V)$. Convergence is defined in Definition 512A, requiring that for any Lipschitz ODE, with appropriate starting procedure $\phi(h)$ and vector $u$, the computed vectors $y^{[n]}$ converge to $u y(x)$. Here $u \in \mathbb{R}^{r}$ is a non-zero vector, $\phi : (0, \infty) \to \mathbb{R}^{r}$ is the starting procedure, $y(x)$ is the exact solution to $y'(x) = f(y(x))$, $y(x_0) = y_0$, and $y^{[n]}$ are the computed vectors over $n$ steps with stepsize $h$.

variables.name: A, U, B, V
variables.definition: matrices defining a general linear method

variables.name: r
variables.definition: integer, dimension of vectors in the method (implied from context of u ∈ R^r)

variables.name: u
variables.definition: non-zero vector in R^r associated with the method

variables.name: φ(h)
variables.definition: starting procedure, φ : (0, ∞) → R^r, for the method

variables.name: y(x)
variables.definition: exact solution to the initial value problem y'(x) = f(y(x)), y(x0) = y0

variables.name: f
variables.definition: function defining the ODE, satisfying a Lipschitz condition

variables.name: y[n]
variables.definition: sequence of vectors computed by the method over n steps with stepsize h

preamble: A general linear method is defined by matrices (A, U, B, V). Convergence is defined in Definition 512A, requiring that for any Lipschitz ODE, with appropriate starting procedure φ(h) and vector u, the computed vectors y[n] converge to uy(x).
